elements.sent-2411-001: Jet suppression and modification is a hallmark feature of heavy-ion collisions.
elements.sent-2411-002: This can be attributed to an accumulated set of effects, including radiative and elastic energy loss and reabsorption of thermalized energy within the jet cone, which are encoded in a quenching weight, determining the probability distribution for a shift of the $\pT$ (energy loss).
elements.sent-2411-003: We perform a data-driven analysis, based on Bayesian inference, to extract information about the energy-loss distribution experienced by propagating jets using generic and flexible parametrizations.
elements.sent-2411-004: We first establish the consistency between different data-sets and, thereby, provide evidence for the universality of the quark/gluon quenching weights for different observables.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we extract that the color dependence of energy loss is slightly bigger than what expected from Casimir scaling, pointing to the importance of multi-parton quenching within high-$\pT$ jets at the LHC.
elements.sent-2411-006: The properties of nuclear matter at high energy and matter densities have wide applications to both cosmological and astrophysical problems.
elements.sent-2411-007: Heavy-ion collisions at the LHC provide sufficient energy per unit volume to surpass the ordinary nuclear density by a factor of 20 \cite{Busza:2018rrf .}
elements.sent-2411-008: There is broad experimental and theoretical consensus that, under these conditions, the constituents of composite hadrons form a strongly-interacting, hot and dense nuclear medium, dubbed the quark-gluon plasma (QGP).
elements.sent-2411-009: This elusive state of matter leaves a characteristic imprint on a number of observables, perhaps most notably on multiparticle flow correlations, which allows to extract non-equilibrium transport coefficients to high precision.
elements.sent-2411-010: The large colliding energies of the nucleons provide, at the same time, conditions where large transverse-momentum exchange processes happen abundantly.
elements.sent-2411-011: These so called ``hard'' processes give rise to the creation of high-$\pT$ QCD jet and heavy particle observables that are systematically described by perturbative QCD and that have often been measured to high precision in colliding systems that do not involve the formation of a QGP.
elements.sent-2411-012: The kinematic conditions at the LHC therefore provide a unique opportunity to study how perturbative QCD behaves in the background of a QGP \cite{Apolinario:2022vzg, Cunqueiro:2021wls .}
elements.sent-2411-013: QCD jets are usually considered well-calibrated probes of the QGP, see \cite{Cunqueiro:2023vxl for a detailed discussion.}
elements.sent-2411-014: But to take a specific example, on the theory side, a first-principle description of jet evolution in matter is a daunting and still largely unresolved challenge.
elements.sent-2411-015: Different computational paradigms invoke a weakly-coupled (or ``perturbative''), such as the BDMPS-Z theory of radiative energy loss, or strongly-coupled, such as the AdS/CFT correspondence, description of the interactions between a jet and a QGP.
elements.sent-2411-016: Furthermore, since both the production of the hard probe as well as the the local conditions of the QGP it traverses are highly stochastic and fluctuating, one often have to rely on Monte Carlo approaches and modeling of various aspects of the collision.
elements.sent-2411-017: It is therefore remarkable that, despite differences in the details of modeling various pieces of the problem, both semi-analytical approaches and sophisticated numerical frameworks are able to describe the basic observables.
elements.sent-2411-018: This raises the question of which common features among the models primarily drive the description of experimental data, and which details have little impact on the final results.
elements.sent-2411-019: One possible answer is that the models are sufficiently flexible, with enough parameters to fit the existing data within experimental uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, it could also be that the conventionally designed observables are not sensitive to the finer details of the underlying physics.
elements.sent-2411-021: A strong argument in favor of this is the inherent bias in jet observables and other similar measures involving a hard momentum scale.
elements.sent-2411-022: Since these observables are rare probes, described by a steeply falling spectrum in transverse momentum $\pT$ or transverse mass $m_T = \sqrt{m^2+\pT^2}$, even small modifications to their kinematics can significantly alter the shape of the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-023: As a result, the experimental samples are dominated by jets that are minimally modified\,\textemdash\, an example of the so-called ``survival bias,'' perhaps for the first time discussed in the context of heavy-ion collisions in \cite{Baier:2001yt and studied further in \cite{Rajagopal:2016uip, Casalderrey-Solana:2018wrw .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: This is for example known to prominently affect the nuclear modification factor $R_{AA}$ which measures the $\pT$ spectrum of jets produced in heavy-ion collisions compared to the equivalent in proton-proton collisions \cite{Baier:2001yt .}
elements.sent-2411-025: There have been many efforts to mitigate these bias effects using machine learning techniques or by defining observables with smaller sensitivity to the initial production spectrum, for example jet substructure observables involving a weak $\pT$-dependence.
elements.sent-2411-026: Another interesting class of observables involves coincidence measurements of leading and subleading jets in opposite hemispheres of the azimuthal angle, quantified for example by the ratio of their transverse momenta.
elements.sent-2411-027: The bias effects mentioned earlier can be mitigated by considering the coincidence measurement of a high-$\pT$ jet with a back-to-back electroweak boson.
elements.sent-2411-028: Since their transverse momenta are balanced at leading order in perturbative QCD, and the electroweak boson does not interact with the QGP, its final $\pT$ serves as a reliable proxy for the initial jet $\pT$\,\textemdash\, that is, the transverse momentum of the parton before it began fragmenting and interacting with the QGP.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, higher-order contributions introduce significant deviations from this idealized back-to-back configuration, leading to a broad distribution.

elements.sent-2411-031: Given the rich experimental data on high-$\pT$ hadrons and fully reconstructed jets, an extensive effort has been dedicated to extract the information about the medium, most succinctly encoded in the so-called jet transport coefficient $\hat q$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Being a measure of the amount of momentum transferred from the medium to the jet, inducing both elastic and inelastic scattering processes, it is directly proportional to (a power of) the energy density of the underlying medium \footnote{At leading order, the momentum transfer is purely transverse to the direction of the jet particles and scales as $\hat q \propto \epsilon^{3/4}$, where $\epsilon$ is the energy density of the medium.. .}

elements.sent-2411-047: Second, after establishing robustness in describing the experimental data, we seek to extract generic features, independent of the specific models used, that give rise to observable trends.

elements.section-2411-002: Jet energy loss in the quark-gluon plasma
elements.sent-2411-079: Partons traversing a hot and dense nuclear medium are affected by elastic and inelastic scattering.
elements.sent-2411-080: For a sufficiently large medium, multiple emissions can take place occur and the radiated quanta can cascade via secondary elastic and inelastic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-081: In course of these processes, a significant energy fraction of the original parton is transferred to particles with energies around the temperature scale of the medium, i.e. $\omega \sim Q_\med$ with $Q_\med \sim T$, and moving at large angles compared to the original direction.
elements.sent-2411-082: Some of the lost energy thermalize with the surrounding medium and can dissipate back into the jet cone \cite{Casalderrey-Solana:2006lmc, Qin:2009uh, Tachibana:2017syd, Chen:2017zte .}
elements.sent-2411-083: The amount of energy ending up outside the cone of the reconstructed jet is referred to as the energy-loss of the same jet.
elements.sent-2411-084: Since for high-$\pT$ jets the elastic energy loss is much smaller than the inelastic energy loss \cite{Qin:2007rn,Cao:2016gvr , we will focus on the latter for the purposes of the discussion in this Section.}
elements.sent-2411-085: However, the hard QCD processes producing energetic particles simultaneously open the phase for emissions due to the collinear and soft divergences of QCD \cite{Mehtar-Tani:2017web,Caucal:2018dla .}
elements.sent-2411-086: For final-state particles, the QCD evolution \cite{Dasgupta:2014yra, Dasgupta:2016bnd, Kang:2016mcy brings the processes at the scale of the hard cross-section $\sim \pT$ down to the scale of the reconstructed jet $Q_\text{ jet \sim \pT R$, where $R$ is the jet cone-angle.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Subsequent splittings inside the jet span a wide range of characteristic formation times, from the initial time-scales of the order $\sim 1/(\pT R)$ to the hadronization time-scale $\sim \pT R/Q_0^2$, where $Q_0 \sim \Lambda_\text{ QCD}$ is the hadronization scale.
elements.sent-2411-088: If the jet scale is large compared to medium scales, i.e. $\pT R \gg Q_\med$, or naively $1/(\pT R) \ll L$ indicating that the emission takes place inside the medium, collinear emissions can take place on time-scales much shorter than the ones typically involved in medium-induced processes.
elements.sent-2411-089: The initially collinear vacuum-like splittings are resolved by the medium when their transverse extent is resolved by multiple-scattering in the medium \cite{Mehtar-Tani:2017web .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The system that starts interacting with the medium is therefore rarely a single parton but rather a partially developed shower consisting of a collimated set of partons.
elements.sent-2411-091: This enhances the effects of energy-loss since each resolved parton acts as an incoherent contributor to the total energy-loss at large angles.
elements.sent-2411-092: For inclusive observables, that only depends on the $\pT$ and $R$ of the measured jets, the information about medium interactions enter only as the amount of lost energy $\varepsilon$ fluctuates on an jet by jet level.
elements.sent-2411-093: Hence, the resulting energy loss distribution $D(\varepsilon)$ depends on a wide range of factors, such as on the initial jet QCD evolution, on the interaction of the jet with the medium, and on the properties of the medium itself, such as its length and temperature.
elements.sent-2411-094: Our two working hypotheses are therefore:.
elements.sent-2411-095: The energy loss distribution is an approximately universal quantity for a range of jet $\pT$, keeping the cone angle $R$ fixed, and for similar medium profiles (kept approximately constant by fixing the centrality class).
elements.sent-2411-096: Jet energy is dominated by Poissonian processes, i.e. independent, stochastic processes which add up to the total energy loss.
elements.sent-2411-097: We will test the first hypothesis against existing experimental data, examining whether the evidence supports or contradicts it.
elements.sent-2411-098: The second hypothesis is instrumental to interpret the color dependence of jet quenching, which is the aim of the second part of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-099: To understand these dependencies, we can start by taking a look at the case where a jet is reduced to a single parton which, under certain assumptions, has a simple theoretical description.
elements.sent-2411-100: Neglecting for the moment elastic contributions, the interaction of a hard parton with the medium induces gluon bremsstrahlung which is responsible for decreasing the energy of the leading parton.
elements.sent-2411-101: This is described by the spectrum $\rmd I/\rmd \omega$, where $\omega$ is the energy of the emitted gluon.
elements.sent-2411-102: We can then construct a probability distribution for a single parton to loose energy $\varepsilon$, which we will denote $D^{(1) (\varepsilon)\equiv\mathcal{D (\varepsilon)$ to distinguish it from the energy-loss distribution of a full jet, see below.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: Assuming that the bremsstrahlung gluons are emitted independently, the all-order energy loss distribution is a sum of Poisson-like contributions, \begin{equation}\label{eq:single-parton-energy-loss} \mathcal{D}(\varepsilon) = \rme^{-\int_0^\infty\!\!\dd\omega\, \frac{\dd I}{\dd\omega}} \sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{1}{n!}\, \prod_{i=1}^n\int_0^\infty\dd\omega_i \, \frac{\dd I}{\dd\omega_i} \, \delta\!\left(\varepsilon-\sum_{i=1}^n\omega_i\right) \,,\end{equation} see also \cite{Baier:2001yt,Salgado:2003gb .}

elements.sent-2411-244: Bayesian inference offers a natural way of accessing and dealing with uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-245: While a frequentist approach gives a point estimate for the parameters $\theta$ of a given model that best describe the observed data $x$, a Bayesian analysis provides us with a full distribution over the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-246: Within a Bayesian analysis, this distribution over the model parameters represents an update of our prior belief about the parameter distribution due to the observed data.
elements.sent-2411-247: This distribution over the model parameters, given the observed data, is thus called posterior distribution, or simply posterior, and is given by the Bayes' theorem, \begin{equation}\label{eq:Bayes} P(\theta|\bm{x}) = \frac{\mathcal{L}(\bm{x}|\theta)P(\theta)}{P(\bm{x})}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-248: In this formula $P(\theta)$ is the prior distribution, which contains previous beliefs about the model parameters, $\mathcal{L (\bm{x |\theta)$ is the likelihood of measuring the observed data $x$ given the model parameters $\theta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Finally, the normalization constant $P(\bm{x ) = \int \rmd \theta \,\mathcal{L (\bm{x |\theta)P(\theta)$ is known as the evidence, which is interpreted as the probability of the data $\bm{x}$ to be observed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-250: For the likelihood, we use a multivariate normal distribution, which is commonly used by assuming normal distributed uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-251: The log-likelihood is then given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:loglikelihood} \ln[\mathcal{L}(\bm{x}|\theta)] = -\frac{1}{2}\ln\left[(2\pi)^n\det\Sigma\right] -\frac{1}{2}\Delta \bm{x}^T\Sigma^{-1}\Delta \bm{x} \,,\end{equation} where $n$ is the number of data points used for inference, $\Sigma$ is a covariance matrix that correlates experimental and model uncertainties, and $\Delta\bm{x = \bm{x _\theta-\bm{x}$, with $\bm{x _\theta$ being the modeled value for $\bm{x}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: The posterior distribution can be used to infer new unobserved data points by computing the respective posterior predictive distribution, i.e. the probability distribution of the new data points.
elements.sent-2411-253: The posterior predictive distribution is computed by marginalizing the likelihood of the new data over the posterior distribution \begin{equation}\label{eq:post_predictive} P(\bm{x}_\text{new}|\bm{x})=\int \rmd \theta \,\mathcal{L}\left(\bm{x}_\text{new}|\theta\right)\,P\left(\theta|\bm{x}\right)\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-254: The posterior distribution of another variable of interest $\psi$, which is not a model parameter, can also be obtained by marginalized the joint probability distribution $P(\psi,\theta|\bm{x )$ of the variable $\psi$ and the model parameters $\theta$ over the posterior distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-255: In the case $\psi$ is a function of the model parameters $\theta$, i.e. $\psi=\psi(\theta)$, then $P(\psi,\theta|\bm{x )=\delta\big(\psi-\psi(\theta)\big) P(\theta|\bm{x )$, and \begin{equation}\label{eq:post_newvar} P(\psi|\bm{x})=\int \rmd \theta\,\delta(\psi-\psi(\theta))\,P(\theta|\bm{x}) \,.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-256: The prior distribution $P(\psi)$ of the variable $\psi$ can also be computed, by simply switching the posterior $P(\theta|\bm{x )$ in Eq.~\ref{eq:post_newvar with the prior $P(\theta)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-257: The posterior distribution contains all the information about the inferred model parameters, and respective uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-258: From it, we can extract quantities such as credible intervals.
elements.sent-2411-259: A particular type of credible interval is the highest density interval (HDI), defined as the narrowest interval of the probability distribution that contains a specified probability mass.
elements.sent-2411-260: Mathematically, the $\alpha$\% HDI of a probability distribution $p(\theta)$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:hdi} \underset{\theta_\text{min}, \theta_\text{max}}{\arg\min}\,\theta_\text{max}-\theta_\text{min}\,:\,\frac{\alpha}{100}=\int_{\theta_\text{min}} ^{\theta_\text{max}} \rmd \theta\, p(\theta)\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-261: Single value estimates can also be computed, such as the maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate, and the moments of the posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-262: The MAP estimate, which corresponds to the mode of the distribution, i.e. its most probable value, is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:MAP} \theta_\text{ MAP} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\,P(\theta|\bm{x})\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-263: The $n^{\rm th}$ moment of the posterior distribution is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:moments} \langle\theta^n\rangle = \int\rmd\theta\,\theta^n\,P(\theta|\bm{x})\,.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-293: To decrease the dimensionality of the space to be interpolated, i.e. the total number of data points, dimensionality reduction is performed via Principal Component Analysis (PCA).

elements.sent-2411-302: Closure tests were performed to ensure the validity and consistency of the inference setup described above.
elements.sent-2411-303: Such tests consist on performing a Bayesian analysis using artificial data, so-called replicas (alternatively as ``mock'' data or pseudo-data).
elements.sent-2411-304: Replicas are generated by computing the model data points for a chosen value of the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-305: Next, a full Bayesian analysis is done, and the resulting posterior distribution is compared with the true parameter distribution, i.e. the distribution used to generate the data in the first place.
elements.sent-2411-306: Ideally, the posterior should be peaked closely to the true value of the chosen parameters, but this depends both on the uncertainties of the emulator and on the sensitivity of the observables to the parameters.
elements.sent-2411-307: Metrics quantifying the success of a closure tests have been discussed in \cite{Weiss:2023yoj,JETSCAPE:2021ehl,JETSCAPE:2023ikg .}
elements.sent-2411-308: In practice, the replicas are generated by numerically computing the full model for a set of random points following a normal distribution in the parameter space, with a chosen mean and standard deviation.
elements.sent-2411-309: This procedure creates a set of values for each data point.
elements.sent-2411-310: The mean of the produced set gives a replica data point, with an uncertainty given by the respective standard deviation.
elements.sent-2411-311: For the Bayesian analysis, a flat prior is used for the model parameters, meaning that the design points of the physical model emulator are drawn from a uniform distribution within the desired range.
elements.sent-2411-312: The range is chosen to be wide enough to explore the preferred values.
elements.sent-2411-313: Bayesian inference is then performed on the generated replica data, using the framework described above to obtain the posterior distributions of the model parameters.

elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Leave-one-out study on the constraining power of each observable
elements.sent-2411-377: The leave-one-out method, widely used in machine learning approaches to validate a specific model, can be used here to assess the constraining power of each individual observable.
elements.sent-2411-378: In this example, inference is performed on all observables but one, and the posterior predictive distribution is computed for both the observed data and the unobserved data, which correspond to the observable left out.
elements.sent-2411-379: We evaluate the constraining power of the observable left out by computing the expectation value and 90\% HDI for the reduced chi-square $\tilde\chi^2$ of the posterior predictive distributions, including both the observed and unobserved data.
elements.sent-2411-380: For a given set of model parameters $\theta$, the reduced chi-square is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:chi-square} \tilde\chi^2(\theta)=\frac{(\bm{x}-\bm{x}_\theta)^2}{\sigma_{\bm{x}}^2} \big/ n_{dof.} \,,\end{equation} where $\bm{x}$ and $\sigma_{\bm{x }$ are the measured data points and respective uncertainties, $\bm{x _\theta$ are the modeled value for $\bm{x}$ for the given $\theta$, and $n_{dof.}$ is the number of degrees of freedom.}}
elements.sent-2411-382: A posterior distribution, and further the expectation value and 90\% HDI, can be computed used Eqs.~\eqref{eq:post_newvar and \eqref{eq:hdi .}}
elements.sent-2411-383: This procedure is repeated for each observable, and the resulting reduced chi-squares are compared.
elements.sent-2411-384: Table~\ref{tab:chi2 shows the reduced chi-squares (expectation value and 90\% HDI) obtained when leaving out one observable at a time for the three energy-loss parametrizations.}
elements.sent-2411-385: The reduced chi-square obtained when no observable is left out is shown as a reference.
elements.sent-2411-386: For all the three parametrizations, leaving out the inclusive jet $R_{AA}$ substantially increases the reduced chi-square.
elements.sent-2411-387: When leaving the $R_{AA}$ out, we also notice a significant widening of the 90\% HDI.
elements.sent-2411-388: We conclude that excluding the $R_{AA}$ reduces the constraining power of the data used for inference.

elements.sent-2411-462: Jet modifications in the dense nuclear environment created during heavy-ion collisions at the LHC serve as valuable tools for probing the quark-gluon plasma.
elements.sent-2411-463: Beyond the ambitious goal of extracting transport properties, one can pose more fundamental questions.
elements.sent-2411-464: For example, what are the generic features of jet suppression when compared to well-established baselines from $pp$ jet physics? Additionally, is the medium sensitive to the partonic composition of the developing QCD jet? We have attempted to answer these questions using a fully data-driven extraction of the so-called energy loss distribution responsible for jet energy loss with minimal assumptions from theory.
elements.sent-2411-465: This has been achieved using Bayesian inference.
elements.sent-2411-466: In contrast to other Bayesian analyses of soft and hard observables in heavy-ion collisions, we have deliberately avoided constraining parameters within a specific model that aims to describe relevant aspects of the observables.
elements.sent-2411-467: Instead, we employ a range of flexible distributions, that satisfy appropriate criteria, to gauge the constraining power of existing experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-468: In order to separate and constrain the color dependence of energy loss, we have analyzed experimental data with different fractions of quark- and gluon-initiated jets in the baseline prediction.
elements.sent-2411-469: An extensive statistical analysis have been performed in order to scrutinize and interpret our numerical results.
elements.sent-2411-470: Our main conclusions can be summarized in the following points.
elements.sent-2411-471: Bias due to the steeply falling nature of the underlying jet spectrum is strongly affecting our ability to constrain the energy loss distribution at all relevant scales.
elements.sent-2411-472: This holds both for observables based on the inclusive jet spectrum as well as for the coincidence photon-jet measurements, although the problem is less severe in the latter case.
elements.sent-2411-473: This has been established through closure tests and the global analysis of experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-474: We have established evidence of universality, i.e. that the extracted energy loss distribution is applicable to different baseline processes under the factorization in Eq.~\eqref{eq:fact .}
elements.sent-2411-475: We have established evidence of color charge dependence of energy loss, see Fig.~\ref{fig:mel_wprior .}
elements.sent-2411-477: We have not been able to extract a characteristic energy scale associated with medium interactions in a finite-size medium.
elements.sent-2411-478: We have established consistency between the analyzed data-sets through a leave-one-out analysis in Sec.~\ref{sec:leave-one-out .}
elements.sent-2411-479: We have also extensively analyzed photon-tagged jet data in Sec.~\ref{sec:other-data and shed light on its sensitivity to nPDF effects.}
elements.sent-2411-480: It seems pertinent to continue to look for strategies to mitigate the inherent bias of jet observables in order to allow experimental data to put more stringent constraints on theoretical descriptions and modeling of jet quenching phenomena in heavy-ion collisions.
